groups.title: Essentials
groups.description: Topic, audience, message, factual input, format, language, length, CTA, and title preferences for a valid first draft.

groups.title: Editorial Shape
groups.description: Angle, reader knowledge level, opening, structure, depth, counterpoints, and ending style.

groups.title: Voice & Author Context
groups.description: Author voice, point of view, writing sample, public context, and terms or styles to avoid.

groups.title: Publishing & Monetization
groups.description: Publication context, access model, free-preview boundary, paid-offer details, subject style, URL slug, and tags.

groups.title: Facts, Sources & Restrictions
groups.description: Evidence, quotations, approved links, disclosures, sensitive-topic context, and additional requirements.

variables.label: Post topic
variables.placeholder: What should the post be about?
variables.hint: Describe the subject, event, question, argument, or idea the post should cover.

help.what: Provide the post topic for this Substack post.
help.why: The model cannot determine the intended subject from the template name alone.
help.example: Good example: Why small creators struggle to stay consistent
help.avoid: Avoid vague placeholders, unsupported claims, broad topics without a clear thesis, or changing the requested post into a different subject.

variables.label: Primary goal
variables.placeholder: Choose the main purpose
variables.hint: Select the single most important outcome the post should achieve.

help.what: Provide the primary goal for this Substack post.
help.why: The same topic requires a different structure depending on the intended reader outcome.
help.example: Good example: Inform or explain
help.avoid: Avoid vague placeholders, unsupported claims, broad topics without a clear thesis, or changing the requested post into a different subject.

variables.label: Target readers
variables.placeholder: Who should read this, and what do they already care about?
variables.hint: Describe the intended readers, their interests, situation, or level of familiarity.

help.what: Provide the target readers for this Substack post.
help.why: Reader knowledge and motivation materially affect examples, terminology, depth, and framing.
help.example: Good example: Solo creators who publish weekly and want a calmer editorial workflow.
help.avoid: Avoid vague placeholders, unsupported claims, broad topics without a clear thesis, or changing the requested post into a different subject.

variables.label: Central message
variables.placeholder: What should readers understand, believe, or reconsider?
variables.hint: State the main argument, conclusion, lesson, or takeaway in plain language.

help.what: Provide the central message for this Substack post.
help.why: A long-form Substack post needs one controlling idea rather than an unfocused collection of points.
help.example: Good example: Consistency improves when creators stop rebuilding their process from scratch.
help.avoid: Avoid vague placeholders, unsupported claims, broad topics without a clear thesis, or changing the requested post into a different subject.

variables.label: Key points
variables.placeholder: List the facts, arguments, examples, or sections that must appear.
variables.hint: Include the information that cannot be safely inferred or omitted.

help.what: Provide the key points for this Substack post.
help.why: It identifies the user-supplied substance that the final post must cover.
help.example: Good example: Templates reduce blank-page friction, saved prompts preserve useful patterns, and simple weekly review keeps the system honest.
help.avoid: Avoid vague placeholders, unsupported claims, broad topics without a clear thesis, or changing the requested post into a different subject.

variables.label: Source material
variables.placeholder: Paste notes, research, drafts, references, interview material, or source excerpts.
variables.hint: Only supplied or independently verified information may be presented as factual.

help.what: Optional context for the source material field.
help.why: It provides factual grounding and lets the template transform existing material without inventing evidence.
help.example: Good example: Use only the supplied workflow notes; do not invent subscriber counts, revenue, or studies.
help.avoid: Avoid invented studies, statistics, quotations, links, citations, attribution, or evidence that was not supplied.

variables.label: Post format
variables.placeholder: Choose a format
variables.hint: Select a format or let the model choose from the goal and material.

help.what: Optional context for the post format field.
help.why: Substack supports several distinct editorial formats that require different content architecture.
help.example: Good example: Essay
help.avoid: Avoid guessed facts, fake urgency, fake scarcity, fabricated social proof, hidden advertising, engagement bait, or unsupported claims.

variables.label: Output language
variables.placeholder: English
variables.hint: Enter the language or regional language variant for the complete output.

help.what: Provide the output language for this Substack post.
help.why: Language cannot always be inferred reliably from the topic or source material.
help.example: Good example: English
help.avoid: Avoid vague placeholders, unsupported claims, broad topics without a clear thesis, or changing the requested post into a different subject.

variables.label: Post length
variables.placeholder: Choose a target length
variables.hint: Select a practical editorial range rather than an exact mandatory word count.

help.what: Optional context for the post length field.
help.why: The intended depth and subscriber commitment materially affect the final post.
help.example: Good example: Standard: 1,000–1,600 words
help.avoid: Avoid guessed facts, fake urgency, fake scarcity, fabricated social proof, hidden advertising, engagement bait, or unsupported claims.

variables.label: Primary reader action
variables.placeholder: Choose one reader action
variables.hint: Select one proportionate action or allow the model to choose.

help.what: Optional context for the primary reader action field.
help.why: A Substack post should support one clear next step rather than several competing conversion goals.
help.example: Good example: Subscribe
help.avoid: Avoid guessed facts, fake urgency, fake scarcity, fabricated social proof, hidden advertising, engagement bait, or unsupported claims.

variables.label: Title direction
variables.placeholder: Example: direct, curious, analytical, understated, or include a phrase
variables.hint: Leave blank to generate accurate platform-appropriate title options automatically.

help.what: Optional context for the title direction field.
help.why: Titles strongly affect editorial positioning and email opens, while remaining optional for users without a preference.
help.example: Good example: Direct and understated, with the phrase content workflow
help.avoid: Avoid guessed facts, fake urgency, fake scarcity, fabricated social proof, hidden advertising, engagement bait, or unsupported claims.

variables.label: Editorial angle
variables.placeholder: What distinctive perspective should shape the post?
variables.hint: Use this for a specific lens, tension, interpretation, or position.

help.what: Optional context for the editorial angle field.
help.why: It helps differentiate the post when the general topic can support several valid approaches.
help.example: Good example: The problem is not motivation; it is asking creators to rebuild the same decisions every week.
help.avoid: Avoid guessed facts, fake urgency, fake scarcity, fabricated social proof, hidden advertising, engagement bait, or unsupported claims.

variables.label: Reader knowledge level
variables.placeholder: Choose reader familiarity
variables.hint: Controls terminology, explanation depth, and assumed background knowledge.

help.what: Optional context for the reader knowledge level field.
help.why: The same argument must be explained differently to beginners and specialists.
help.example: Good example: General audience
help.avoid: Avoid guessed facts, fake urgency, fake scarcity, fabricated social proof, hidden advertising, engagement bait, or unsupported claims.

variables.label: Opening approach
variables.placeholder: Choose how the post begins
variables.hint: Controls the first impression without requiring the user to write the opening.

help.what: Optional context for the opening approach field.
help.why: The opening has a major effect on reader retention and may depend on the author’s preferred style.
help.example: Good example: Reader question
help.avoid: Avoid guessed facts, fake urgency, fake scarcity, fabricated social proof, hidden advertising, engagement bait, or unsupported claims.

variables.label: Structure preference
variables.placeholder: Choose a content flow
variables.hint: Select a preferred progression or allow the model to build the most natural structure.

help.what: Optional context for the structure preference field.
help.why: Structure materially changes how a long-form post develops and how easily it can be followed.
help.example: Good example: Problem and response
help.avoid: Avoid guessed facts, fake urgency, fake scarcity, fabricated social proof, hidden advertising, engagement bait, or unsupported claims.

variables.label: Content depth
variables.placeholder: Choose the level of depth
variables.hint: Controls context, nuance, examples, and treatment of implications.

help.what: Optional context for the content depth field.
help.why: Word count alone does not specify the expected analytical or explanatory depth.
help.example: Good example: Balanced
help.avoid: Avoid guessed facts, fake urgency, fake scarcity, fabricated social proof, hidden advertising, engagement bait, or unsupported claims.

variables.label: Address counterpoints
variables.placeholder: Not applicable
variables.hint: Include a strong objection, limitation, trade-off, or alternative explanation when relevant.

help.what: Optional context for the address counterpoints field.
help.why: Some essays benefit from explicit counterarguments, while others become unnecessarily diluted by them.
help.example: Good example: On
help.avoid: Avoid guessed facts, fake urgency, fake scarcity, fabricated social proof, hidden advertising, engagement bait, or unsupported claims.

variables.label: Ending style
variables.placeholder: Choose how the post closes
variables.hint: Controls the emotional and editorial effect of the final paragraphs.

help.what: Optional context for the ending style field.
help.why: A reflective essay and a practical guide require different types of closure.
help.example: Good example: Practical next step
help.avoid: Avoid guessed facts, fake urgency, fake scarcity, fabricated social proof, hidden advertising, engagement bait, or unsupported claims.

variables.label: Tone
variables.placeholder: Choose up to three qualities; or list up to three qualities separated by commas.
variables.hint: Select only the qualities that should be consistently noticeable. Available options: Clear and natural, Conversational, Analytical, Reflective, Warm, Direct, Witty, Serious, Provocative but fair.

help.what: Optional context for the tone field.
help.why: Substack is strongly author-driven, so tone materially affects whether the result feels authentic.
help.example: Good example: Clear and natural, reflective
help.avoid: Avoid guessed facts, fake urgency, fake scarcity, fabricated social proof, hidden advertising, engagement bait, or unsupported claims.

variables.label: Point of view
variables.placeholder: Choose a narrative perspective
variables.hint: First person should be used only when supported by supplied author context.

help.what: Optional context for the point of view field.
help.why: Perspective changes the relationship between the writer, subject, and subscriber.
help.example: Good example: Mixed editorial voice
help.avoid: Avoid guessed facts, fake urgency, fake scarcity, fabricated social proof, hidden advertising, engagement bait, or unsupported claims.

variables.label: Writing sample
variables.placeholder: Paste a short sample of the author’s natural writing.
variables.hint: The model should follow its broad rhythm and formality without copying phrases.

help.what: Optional context for the writing sample field.
help.why: A supplied sample provides more reliable voice adaptation than numerous abstract style controls.
help.example: Good example: I like practical essays that make one useful distinction and then show how it changes the work.
help.avoid: Avoid fake first-person experience, invented credentials, copied private wording, or details the author would not publish.

variables.label: Author context
variables.placeholder: Relevant experience, perspective, role, relationship, or personal context
variables.hint: Include only details the author is comfortable presenting publicly.

help.what: Optional context for the author context field.
help.why: It allows credible first-person framing without fabricating expertise or personal experience.
help.example: Good example: Solo founder building Creatornivo for content workflows.
help.avoid: Avoid fake first-person experience, invented credentials, copied private wording, or details the author would not publish.

variables.label: Terms or styles to avoid
variables.placeholder: List unwanted phrases, clichés, framing, terminology, or stylistic habits.
variables.hint: Use one field for all brand, voice, and wording restrictions.

help.what: Optional context for the terms or styles to avoid field.
help.why: Specific prohibitions cannot always be inferred from positive tone selections.
help.example: Good example: game-changing, unlock the power of, in today's fast-paced world
help.avoid: Avoid guessed facts, fake urgency, fake scarcity, fabricated social proof, hidden advertising, engagement bait, or unsupported claims.

variables.label: Publication context
variables.placeholder: Publication name, subject area, recurring series, or subscriber expectations
variables.hint: Provide only context that should subtly influence the post and packaging.

help.what: Optional context for the publication context field.
help.why: A post may need to fit an established publication, series, or reader relationship.
help.example: Good example: Creatornivo Notes, a publication about practical creator workflows
help.avoid: Avoid guessed facts, fake urgency, fake scarcity, fabricated social proof, hidden advertising, engagement bait, or unsupported claims.

variables.label: Post access
variables.placeholder: Choose who can read the post
variables.hint: Controls whether the output includes paid-reader framing or a free-preview marker.

help.what: Optional context for the post access field.
help.why: Free, paid, and preview-based posts require materially different content boundaries and CTAs.
help.example: Good example: Free
help.avoid: Avoid invented paid benefits, prices, deadlines, discounts, exclusivity, scarcity, trial terms, or subscriber perks.

variables.label: Free-preview boundary
variables.placeholder: Describe what free readers should see before the paid section begins.
variables.hint: Leave blank to place the break after genuine value but before the strongest paid material.

help.what: Optional context for the free-preview boundary field.
help.why: The author may have a deliberate editorial or conversion boundary for free subscribers.
help.example: Good example: Free readers should get the problem and first practical distinction before the paid framework.
help.avoid: Avoid guessed facts, fake urgency, fake scarcity, fabricated social proof, hidden advertising, engagement bait, or unsupported claims.

variables.label: Paid subscriber value
variables.placeholder: What do paid subscribers genuinely receive?
variables.hint: Include only real benefits, frequency, access, or membership details.

help.what: Optional context for the paid subscriber value field.
help.why: Any paid-upgrade language must be based on real supplied benefits rather than invented perks.
help.example: Good example: Paid subscribers receive the complete workflow breakdown and monthly source notes.
help.avoid: Avoid invented paid benefits, prices, deadlines, discounts, exclusivity, scarcity, trial terms, or subscriber perks.

variables.label: Email subject style
variables.placeholder: Choose an inbox style
variables.hint: Controls subject-line positioning without permitting deceptive framing.

help.what: Optional context for the email subject style field.
help.why: Substack posts are also email messages, making inbox presentation part of the native output.
help.example: Good example: Direct and clear
help.avoid: Avoid guessed facts, fake urgency, fake scarcity, fabricated social proof, hidden advertising, engagement bait, or unsupported claims.

variables.label: Preferred URL slug
variables.placeholder: example-post-slug
variables.hint: Leave blank to generate a short lowercase slug from the recommended title.

help.what: Optional context for the preferred url slug field.
help.why: A custom post URL may be important for clarity, sharing, and long-term discovery.
help.example: Good example: creator-content-workflow
help.avoid: Avoid guessed facts, fake urgency, fake scarcity, fabricated social proof, hidden advertising, engagement bait, or unsupported claims.

variables.label: Tags or discovery terms
variables.placeholder: Example: independent media, creator economy, online publishing
variables.hint: Add focused terms only; the output will suggest no more than five tags.

help.what: Optional context for the tags or discovery terms field.
help.why: The author may use an existing publication taxonomy that cannot be inferred reliably.
help.example: Good example: creator economy, publishing systems, content workflow
help.avoid: Avoid guessed facts, fake urgency, fake scarcity, fabricated social proof, hidden advertising, engagement bait, or unsupported claims.

variables.label: Claims and evidence
variables.placeholder: List important claims with the evidence, data, or source supporting each one.
variables.hint: Unsupported material claims will be softened, omitted, or flagged.

help.what: Optional context for the claims and evidence field.
help.why: It separates factual support from general notes and reduces invented or overstated claims.
help.example: Good example: Claim: blank-page work creates repeat friction. Evidence: supplied product notes and user workflow observations only.
help.avoid: Avoid invented studies, statistics, quotations, links, citations, attribution, or evidence that was not supplied.

variables.label: Quotes and attribution
variables.placeholder: Paste approved quotes with speaker, source, and context.
variables.hint: A paraphrase will not be converted into a direct quotation.

help.what: Optional context for the quotes and attribution field.
help.why: Quotation wording and attribution require exact user-supplied information.
help.example: Good example: Quote from approved interview notes: “The hard part is starting from zero every Monday.”
help.avoid: Avoid invented studies, statistics, quotations, links, citations, attribution, or evidence that was not supplied.

variables.label: Links to include
variables.placeholder: Paste approved URLs with a note explaining where each should lead.
variables.hint: No links or destinations will be invented.

help.what: Optional context for the links to include field.
help.why: The model must know the exact approved destinations for references or conversion actions.
help.example: Good example: https://www.creatornivo.com/resources — creator workflow resources
help.avoid: Avoid invented studies, statistics, quotations, links, citations, attribution, or evidence that was not supplied.

variables.label: Disclosure information
variables.placeholder: Sponsorship, affiliate, employer, product, client, or other material relationship
variables.hint: Relevant commercial or personal interests should be disclosed clearly.

help.what: Optional context for the disclosure information field.
help.why: Transparent affiliation cannot be inferred and may be necessary for reader trust or compliance.
help.example: Good example: Disclosure: I created Creatornivo.
help.avoid: Avoid hiding material relationships or implying independent neutrality when a sponsorship, affiliate, employer, product, client, or ownership connection exists.

variables.label: Sensitive topic context
variables.placeholder: Jurisdiction, risk, affected group, uncertainty, or wording that needs special care
variables.hint: Use for health, legal, financial, political, safety, child-related, or regulated topics.

help.what: Optional context for the sensitive topic context field.
help.why: High-stakes subjects may require jurisdictional or contextual details that cannot be safely inferred.
help.example: Good example: Avoid personal productivity claims that imply guaranteed mental-health or business outcomes.
help.avoid: Avoid personalized medical, legal, financial, political, safety, tax, or regulated advice unless the supplied context supports safe general wording.

variables.label: Additional requirements
variables.placeholder: Add any remaining instructions that materially affect the final result.
variables.hint: Use this single field for custom length, formatting, required wording, or special restrictions.

help.what: Optional context for the additional requirements field.
help.why: It supports legitimate uncommon requirements without creating numerous low-value fields.
help.example: Good example: Use Markdown headings sparingly and avoid fake urgency, fake social proof, and invented metrics.
help.avoid: Avoid guessed facts, fake urgency, fake scarcity, fabricated social proof, hidden advertising, engagement bait, or unsupported claims.
